description:
<![CDATA[
Context
--------
You are a cloud-architect who is getting ready to migrate several containerised micro-services to a new Kubernetes cluster.  
Before the cut-over you want to know which services are invoked most frequently so that you can provision the right amount of compute for each one.

What already exists
-------------------
1. A directory **/home/user/migration_logs** that contains exactly three plain-text log files:
   • **day1.log**  
   • **day2.log**  
   • **day3.log**

   Each file is one service-name per line, all in lower-case with *no* surrounding spaces or extra columns.

2. You have normal user privileges (no root).

Your objectives
---------------
A. Create (if it does not already exist) a new directory  
   **/home/user/migration_summary**

B. Inside that directory build a CSV report called  
   **service_frequency_20240101.csv**

   File format that will be tested:

   • Line-1 (header) must be the literal string  
     `SERVICE_NAME,INSTANCE_COUNT`

   • Starting on line-2, list every unique service that appears in *any* of the three source logs.

   • Each subsequent line must be in the exact form  
     `service_name,count`  
     where  
       – *service_name* is the lower-case service name as it appears in the logs.  
       – *count* is the total number of times that service appears across **all three** logs.

   • Sorting requirements  
       1. Descending by the numeric *count*.  
       2. If two or more services have the same count, order them alphabetically (ASCII/lexicographic) by the service name.

   • There must be **no leading or trailing spaces**, no quotes, no extra columns, and a single trailing newline at the very end of the file.

C. After creating the CSV, print the three most frequent services to standard output in this exact format (the automated grader only checks that you *did* echo something; the exact wording is up to you, but printing them makes the task more realistic).

Constraints & Hints
-------------------
• You are encouraged to use classic UNIX pipelines built around  
  `sort`, `uniq -c`, `cut`, `awk`, etc.  
  Try doing the heavy lifting with a single pipeline rather than multiple temporary files.

• Remember that `uniq` only collapses adjacent identical lines, so combine it with `sort` first.

• The grader will look **only** at the existence and exact contents of  
  `/home/user/migration_summary/service_frequency_20240101.csv`.

Success criteria
----------------
Your solution will be considered correct when:

1. The file `/home/user/migration_summary/service_frequency_20240101.csv` exists.
2. Its contents match the specification above *exactly* (line order, commas, header, newline).
3. The top-3 services are echoed to stdout during your session (visual confirmation; not graded for exact wording).

Good luck – ship it!]]>
truth:
<![CDATA[
PRE-EXISTING DIRECTORIES & FILES
--------------------------------
/home/user/migration_logs
    ├── day1.log        (8 lines)
    ├── day2.log        (9 lines)
    └── day3.log       (11 lines)

Exact lines of each file (newline after every line):

day1.log
--------
auth
billing
auth
user
search
billing
auth
cdn


day2.log
--------
search
video
auth
search
billing
user
video
video
cdn


day3.log
--------
auth
billing
search
search
cdn
cdn
billing
auth
video
video
analytics


GROUND-TRUTH COUNTS ACROSS ALL THREE FILES
------------------------------------------
auth       6
billing    5
search     5
video      5
cdn        4
user       2
analytics  1


EXPECTED OUTPUT FILE
--------------------
Path: /home/user/migration_summary/service_frequency_20240101.csv
Contents (including header and final newline):

SERVICE_NAME,INSTANCE_COUNT
auth,6
billing,5
search,5
video,5
cdn,4
user,2
analytics,1

(The file must terminate with a single '\n' right after "analytics,1")

The grader will verify:
  • that the file exists at the exact path,
  • that its SHA-256 sums to the value computed from the block above,
  • that both line-order and byte-content are identical.

No other artefacts are required, but any additional files created by the agent must not interfere with normal user permissions (rw-r--r-- at most).
]]>
